title: Biome Connections Network
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive network graph showing material and energy flow connections between 13 terrestrial and aquatic biomes with disruption simulation

learningObjectives: Assess how material and energy flows connect different biomes across the planet
Identify types of connections between biomes including migration, water flow, nutrient transport, and atmospheric connections
Predict cascading effects when one biome is disrupted
Evaluate which biomes are most critical to global ecological stability
prerequisites: Knowledge of major terrestrial and aquatic biome types
Understanding of food webs and energy flow
Basic familiarity with migration and nutrient cycling

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Network visualization with disruption simulation for systems thinking
keyQuestions: Which biome has the most connections to other biomes?
What happens to distant biomes when a tropical rainforest is disrupted?
How do atmospheric connections differ from direct material flows?
Why must conservation efforts consider biome connectivity rather than protecting individual biomes in isolation?
commonMisconceptions: Biomes are independent and isolated from each other (they are connected by multiple material and energy flows)
Only adjacent biomes affect each other (bird migration and atmospheric transport connect distant biomes)
Aquatic and terrestrial biomes are separate systems (they are connected through water cycles, nutrient runoff, and migration)
assessmentOpportunities: Have students rank biomes by connectivity and predict which disruption would cause the most cascading damage
Ask students to trace a specific material flow (e.g., nutrients from forests to oceans) through multiple connected biomes
Challenge students to design a global conservation strategy that prioritizes the most connected biomes
